management_tone.classification: confident and constructive with pragmatic caution
management_tone.change_vs_prior: More outwardly confident and momentum-focused in the current quarter versus a defensive, stress-tested tone in the prior period. Management shifted from framing 2025 as a year of testing and recovery to highlighting concrete early-quarter improvements (volumes, net revenue per hectoliter, cash flow) while maintaining cautious language on macro/cost risks.
justification_quotes: In tough moments, great companies and cultures find a way to get stronger, and that is what this quarter begins to show.

2025 was a tough season to play with a wet beach, cold weather and a game that kept changing. It forced us to build muscle, resilience and adaptability, and it strengthened our collective ownership as a team.

guidance_changes.summary: No change to the headline Brazil Beer cash COGS per hectoliter guidance (4.5%-7.5% increase for 2026). Company reiterates ambition to expand consolidated margin and maintains fiscal discipline, while flagging cost pressures should ease after Q1 and highlighting a stronger cash generation start to the year.
changes_vs_prior: Brazil Beer cash COGS per hectoliter guidance unchanged at an increase of 4.5% to 7.5% for 2026 (same range reiterated vs prior commentary).
No change to the stated ambition of expanding consolidated margin over time; management reiterates continued share buybacks and IOC payments as part of capital return priorities.
themes_vs_prior: Continued emphasis on revenue management and premiumization as drivers of nr/hl and margin expansion; carryover from 2025 revenue management is cited as a key contributor to Q1 performance.
Greater emphasis on the digital ecosystem (Zé Delivery, BEES) as growth and activation engines; stronger cash generation is being used to fund reinvestment and shareholder returns.

evidence_quotes: In essence, Zé gives us a direct window into the future... This portfolio supported a solid net revenue per hectoliter performance, up 8.3% in the quarter.
First, net revenue per hectoliter growth of 7.5%, supported by stronger brands, revenue management strategy and continued premiumization across our portfolio...

objective_metrics.prior_period_value: 50 basis points (consolidated organic EBITDA margin expansion in 2025)
objective_metrics.current_period_value: 60 basis points (Q1 2026 normalized EBITDA margin expansion)
objective_metrics.delta: +10 basis points (quarterly reported expansion vs prior-year full-year expansion metric)
evidence_quotes: Top line grew 9.6% and EBITDA grew 7.6% despite cost headwinds from FX and commodities.... we delivered double-digit EBITDA growth with margin expansion of 60 basis points.
we expanded organic EBITDA margin by 50 basis points, marking our third consecutive year of margin expansion...

objective_metrics.prior_period_value: 6.1% increase (full-year 2025 Brazil Beer cash COGS per hectoliter, noted as landing in the first quartile of guidance)
objective_metrics.current_period_value: 9% increase consolidated; Brazil Beer up 14.6% in Q1 2026
objective_metrics.delta: Consolidated: +2.9 percentage points vs prior (Brazil Beer: materially higher in Q1 vs prior full-year point)
evidence_quotes: Consolidated cash COGS per hectoliter excluding marketplace, increased 9% in the period, with Brazil Beer up 14.6%, reflecting the expected pressure from FX and commodities headwinds.
our cash COGS per hectoliter, excluding non-Ambev marketplace products increased by 6.1% in 2025 at the lowest quartile of our guidance.

top_analyst_questions.question: My question is about net revenue per hectoliter in Brazil Beer... If you could qualify the 8% year-on-year increase, what drove that in terms of the contribution from mix? Any contribution from price increases... and anything different in this ICMS dynamic this year that may also, in part, explain the pricing performance in Brazil Beer?
top_analyst_questions.response_summary: Management broke the nr/hl increase into three components: carryover from 2025 revenue management actions (easier comp vs 2024->25), continued execution of the revenue management agenda in 2026, and mix (above-core segment growth). They also said there was nothing notable on the tax (ICMS) side versus last year.
top_analyst_questions.response_quality: High — extracted a clear, hierarchical decomposition (carryover; revenue management ongoing; mix) and a direct confirmation that taxes/ICMS were not a material factor in the quarter.
evidence_quotes: the first component is exactly the -- all the effort put behind our revenue management agenda in '25 and the carryover we brought into the first quarter of '26.
And just to complement here, Henrique, Fleury. On the tax side, there was nothing that was important to highlight during the quarter. So same as last year.

top_analyst_questions.question: I just wanted to follow up a little bit on the COGS outlook... given the current environment with higher aluminum prices, etc. How should we think about the cost flow through for you guys based on the hedges for the rest of the year?
top_analyst_questions.response_summary: Management reiterated the 2026 Brazil Beer cash COGS per hectoliter guidance of +4.5% to +7.5%, said Q1 is likely the peak for cost pressure and that costs should start easing from Q2, and emphasized ongoing productivity and efficiency initiatives plus the role of hedges and visibility.
top_analyst_questions.response_quality: Medium-High — management gave explicit guidance confirmation, called out cadence (Q1 peak, easing from Q2) and reiterated actions, but did not provide a granular hedge/commodity sensitivity schedule.
evidence_quotes: When we're starting '26, what we have said to the market is our range between 4.5% for Brazil Beer cash COGS per hectoliter, excluding marketplace from 4.5% to 7.5%.
We know, and that's something that I also comment that we know that Q1 is probably the highest on cost, and that should start easing through the second quarter onwards.

top_analyst_questions.question: How do you think about the World Cup timeline for demand to flow through your P&L? And are you already seeing any early pricing movements from competitors ahead of this demand peak?
top_analyst_questions.response_summary: Management quantified historical World Cup contribution (~0.3%-0.4% industry growth annually), stated the main impact occurs in Q2-Q3 (when the event takes place), noted that preparatory activations can show up earlier, and emphasized use of digital platforms (Zé Delivery/BEES) to activate demand. They did not report competitor pre-pricing actions.
top_analyst_questions.response_quality: High — provided a concrete historical contribution estimate and clear timing expectations (Q2–Q3), while also emphasizing activations and digital leverage; limited detail on competitor pricing dynamics (answered primarily on timing and activation).
evidence_quotes: historically, the World Cup usually brings a contribution of around 0.3% to 0.4% industry growth year-over-year... That contribution usually impacts as well the second to third quarters.
And just to add one thing here, Lisboa, I think it's important to remember that on this World Cup, different from the prior, our digital platforms are much more evolved.

red_flags.quote: I will keep the protocol. I'm going to answer 1 question to give you all the chance to interact with us, okay?
red_flags.rationale: Management explicitly limited answers to a single question during the Q&A, which prevents follow-up and can be used to avoid addressing multi-part or difficult questions.

red_flags.quote: I cannot -- this is not a guidance. I cannot tell you how that will come over the year.
red_flags.rationale: When asked about timing/structure of dividends/capital returns, the CFO declined to provide forward detail, using a direct refusal that leaves timing and amounts ambiguous for investors.

red_flags.quote: I'm going to answer 1 question to give you all the chance to interact with us, okay? So regarding your first question, World Cup contribution, look, historically, the World Cup usually brings a contribution of around 0.3% to 0.4% industry growth...
red_flags.rationale: Management shifted the scope of the Q&A to a single topic and moved into a prepared historical framing on World Cup contribution rather than addressing the multi-part, contemporaneous concerns about competitor pricing and early signals—effectively changing the subject to a safer narrative.

items: Brazil Beer volumes outperformed in the quarter (Ambev volumes +1.2% in Brazil Beer) despite industry softness commentary earlier in the cycle.
Very strong operating cash flow: "strongest first quarter performance in the past 10 years" and cash flow from operating activities totaled BRL 3.2 billion (BRL +2 billion YoY).
Consolidated cash COGS per hectoliter rose materially in Q1 (9% consolidated; Brazil Beer +14.6%), a notable near-term cost pressure even as guidance was reiterated.
surprise_score.justification: The quarter contained a mix of positive execution surprises (nr/hl up 8.3%, Brazil Beer volumes +1.2%, exceptionally strong Q1 operating cash flow) and a material near-term cost spike (COGS/hl up notably in Q1). Guidance was reiterated (no downside surprise), so the net impact is a moderate surprise level: results show clearer early momentum but also confirm short-term cost pressure — sufficient to move near-term expectations but not to change the investment thesis.
